question: Explain the box model and box-sizing.
answer:
Every element is a box: content → padding → border → margin. By default (content-box) width applies to content only, so padding/border add to the total size.

box-sizing: border-box makes width include padding and border — far more predictable, which is why most resets set it globally.
tip: * { box-sizing: border-box } is the first line of almost every CSS reset.
deep:
The four layers, inside out

  • Content — the text/image; its size is width × height.
  • Padding — space inside the border, takes the background.
  • Border — the edge around the padding.
  • Margin — transparent space outside, separating this box from others.

content-box vs border-box — the whole point

.a { box-sizing: content-box; width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 5px solid; }
/* rendered width = 200 + 20*2 + 5*2 = 250px  😖 */

.b { box-sizing: border-box; width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 5px solid; }
/* rendered width = 200px  ✅ padding & border fit INSIDE */

With the default content-box, padding and border are added to your declared width, so a "200px" box is actually wider. border-box makes width the final rendered width — which is why nearly every reset sets it globally.

*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; }

Margin doesn't count toward width — and it collapses

Margin is always outside the box and never part of width. Vertical margins between block elements also collapse: adjacent 20px and 30px margins produce a 30px gap, not 50px (padding and border never collapse).

Handy diagnostics

  • outline draws like a border but takes no space — great for debugging layout.
  • DevTools' box-model panel shows the four layers with live numbers.

How to say it in an interview

“Every element is a box of content, padding, border, and margin. By default width only sizes the content, so padding and border add to the total — I set box-sizing: border-box globally so width is the actual rendered width. Margin stays outside the box and collapses vertically.”

question: Flexbox vs Grid — when to use which?
answer:
Flexbox is one-dimensional (a row or a column) — great for toolbars, nav bars, aligning items along one axis.

Grid is two-dimensional (rows and columns together) — great for page layouts and card galleries.
tip: Flex for components in a line; Grid for the overall page structure.
code: .cards { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(240px, 1fr)); gap: 16px; }

question: How does specificity work?
answer: The cascade picks the winning rule by specificity: inline styles > IDs > classes/attributes/pseudo-classes > elements. Ties go to the later rule. !important overrides all of it (avoid it).
tip: If a style won't apply, you're usually losing a specificity battle — inspect the computed styles.

question: Explain position: relative / absolute / fixed / sticky.
answer:
• relative — offset from its normal spot; establishes a positioning context.
  • absolute — removed from flow, positioned to the nearest positioned ancestor.
  • fixed — positioned to the viewport, stays on scroll.
  • sticky — relative until it hits a threshold, then sticks.
tip: sticky needs a top/left value and a scrollable parent to work.

question: px vs em vs rem vs %, vh/vw.
answer:
• px — absolute pixels.
  • em — relative to the parent's font size (compounds).
  • rem — relative to the root font size (predictable — use for spacing/typography).
  • % — relative to the parent's size.
  • vh/vw — 1% of viewport height/width.
tip: Default to rem for scalable, accessible sizing.

question: How do you center an element?
answer:
Modern answer: flexbox or grid.

  • Flex: display:flex; justify-content:center; align-items:center;
  • Grid: display:grid; place-items:center;
  • Absolute: top:50%; left:50%; transform:translate(-50%,-50%);
tip: place-items: center is the shortest way to center in both axes.

question: Pseudo-class vs pseudo-element.
answer:
Pseudo-class (:hover, :focus, :nth-child) targets a state of an element.

Pseudo-element (::before, ::after, ::placeholder) targets a generated/sub part of an element.
tip: One colon for pseudo-classes, two for pseudo-elements.

question: What are CSS custom properties (variables)?
answer: Runtime variables defined with --name and read with var(--name). They cascade and inherit, can be changed with JS, and are the standard way to implement theming (light/dark) without a preprocessor.
tip: Redefine the same variables under a [data-theme="dark"] selector to switch themes instantly.

question: How do you build a responsive layout?
answer: Use fluid units (%, rem, fr), flexible layouts (flex/grid with minmax/auto-fill), the viewport meta tag, and media queries for breakpoints. Prefer a mobile-first approach: base styles for small screens, then min-width queries to enhance.
tip: Modern grids with auto-fill + minmax often remove the need for media queries entirely.

question: Explain the combinators: space, >, +, ~.
answer:
• A B — descendant (any level).
  • A > B — direct child.
  • A + B — immediate next sibling.
  • A ~ B — any following sibling.
tip: Prefer > (direct child) to keep styles scoped and avoid leaking into nested components.

question: Why doesn't my z-index work? (stacking context)
answer: z-index only compares elements within the same stacking context. Properties like transform, opacity < 1, filter, and position + z-index create new contexts, trapping children's z-index inside them.
tip: A parent's transform can make a huge child z-index useless — check ancestors first.

question: Which properties are cheap to animate?
answer: Animate transform and opacity — the browser can offload them to the GPU (the compositor) without reflow/repaint. Avoid animating width, height, top, or left, which trigger layout on every frame.
tip: 'transform + opacity only' is the golden rule for smooth 60fps animation.

question: What is BEM and why use a naming convention?
answer: BEM (Block__Element--Modifier) is a class-naming convention that keeps CSS flat, predictable, and low-specificity, avoiding deep selector chains and accidental overrides — helpful on large teams.
tip: BEM keeps specificity flat so styles are easy to override intentionally.

question: Explain flex-grow, flex-shrink, and flex-basis.
answer:
The flex shorthand controls how items share space:

  • flex-grow — how much an item grows to fill free space.
  • flex-shrink — how much it shrinks when space is tight.
  • flex-basis — the starting size before grow/shrink.

flex: 1 = 1 1 0 (grow equally from zero).
tip: flex: 1 on children makes them share the row equally — the most-used flex value.
deep:
Two axes: main and cross

Flexbox is one-dimensional. flex-direction sets the main axis (row = horizontal, column = vertical); the cross axis is perpendicular. All the alignment properties are defined relative to these axes, which is the thing to get straight first.

PropertyAxisDoes
justify-contentmaindistributes items along the main axis
align-itemscrossaligns items on the cross axis
align-contentcrossspaces multiple lines (when wrapped)
align-selfcrossoverrides align-items for one item

The three flex-item properties

  • flex-grow — a unitless ratio of how leftover free space is shared. 0 = don't grow. Two items at 1 and 2 split extra space 1:2.
  • flex-shrink — ratio for how items give up space when the container is too small. 0 = never shrink (can overflow).
  • flex-basis — the item's size along the main axis before grow/shrink. auto uses the content/width; 0 ignores it.

Reading the flex shorthand

flex: 1;        /* 1 1 0   — grow & shrink from zero, equal columns */
flex: auto;     /* 1 1 auto — grow/shrink from content size */
flex: none;     /* 0 0 auto — fixed, never grow or shrink */
flex: 0 0 200px;/* fixed 200px track */

Key nuance: flex: 1 uses basis 0, so items become truly equal regardless of content. flex: auto uses basis auto, so bigger content starts bigger. That difference trips people up constantly.

Common patterns

/* perfect centering */
.center { display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }

/* push last item to the right (nav bar) */
.nav { display: flex; gap: 12px; }
.nav .spacer { margin-left: auto; }

/* responsive wrap */
.cards { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 16px; }
.cards > * { flex: 1 1 240px; } /* grow, but never below ~240px */

Gotchas

  • Items overflow instead of shrinking? A child's min-width defaults to auto (min-content) — set min-width: 0 to let it shrink.
  • margin: auto on a flex item absorbs free space — handy for pushing items apart.
  • Use gap for spacing, not margins — it's cleaner and doesn't add edge gaps.

How to say it in an interview

“Flexbox lays items along a main axis set by flex-direction; justify-content aligns on the main axis and align-items on the cross axis. On items, flex-grow/shrink are ratios for sharing or giving up space and flex-basis is the starting size — flex: 1 means grow and shrink from a zero basis, giving equal columns.”

question: How do grid-template-areas work?
answer: Name regions of a grid with an ASCII-art map, then place children by area name. It makes complex page layouts readable and easy to rearrange responsively (just redefine the areas in a media query).
tip: Template areas make responsive layout re-flows a one-property change.

question: What does clamp() do (fluid typography)?
answer: clamp(MIN, PREFERRED, MAX) returns the preferred value bounded by a min and max — perfect for fluid type/spacing that scales with the viewport but never gets too small or too large, often removing media queries.
tip: clamp with a vw-based middle value gives smooth responsive headings.

question: How do you keep an element's aspect ratio?
answer: The aspect-ratio property reserves space in a fixed ratio (e.g. 16/9) so the box sizes correctly and avoids layout shift — replacing the old padding-top hack. Great for video/image placeholders.
tip: aspect-ratio prevents cumulative layout shift (CLS) for media before it loads.

question: What does object-fit do?
answer: Controls how a replaced element (img/video) fills its box: cover (fill, crop), contain (fit, letterbox), fill (stretch). Pair with object-position to control the crop focus.
tip: object-fit: cover is the CSS way to crop images to a fixed box without distortion.

question: What are container queries and how do they differ from media queries?
answer: Container queries style an element based on the size of its container, not the viewport — so a component can adapt wherever it's placed (sidebar vs main). Truly reusable, context-aware components.
tip: Container queries make a card component responsive regardless of where it's dropped.

question: What is the :has() selector?
answer: The long-awaited parent selector: style an element based on its descendants or following siblings. E.g. style a card that contains an image, or a label whose input is invalid — logic that previously required JavaScript.
tip: :has() finally lets you style a parent based on its children — no JS needed.

question: What are cascade layers (@layer)?
answer: @layer lets you group styles into ordered layers (e.g. reset, base, components, utilities). Layer order wins over specificity, so you can manage the cascade predictably and stop fighting !important in large codebases.
tip: Layers let a low-specificity utility beat a high-specificity component rule intentionally.
code:
@layer reset, base, components;
@layer components { .btn { color: red; } }

question: What are logical properties (margin-inline, inset)?
answer: Direction-aware properties that adapt to writing mode/text direction: margin-inline/block, padding-inline, inset. They make layouts work automatically in RTL languages without separate stylesheets.
tip: Use margin-inline instead of margin-left/right for RTL-ready layouts.

question: What does will-change do, and when should you avoid it?
answer: will-change hints the browser to promote an element to its own GPU layer ahead of an animation, reducing jank. But overusing it wastes memory and can hurt performance — apply it sparingly, ideally just before animating, and remove it after.
tip: Don't slap will-change on everything — it's a targeted hint, not a global optimisation.

question: display:none vs visibility:hidden vs opacity:0 — how do they differ?
answer:
Takes space?Clickable?Screen readers?
display:noneno (removed from layout)nono
visibility:hiddenyes (space reserved)nono
opacity:0yesyes (still there)yes
tip: opacity:0 is invisible but still clickable — a common source of 'ghost' click bugs.
code:
.gone   { display: none; }      /* no space, not rendered */
.hidden { visibility: hidden; } /* space kept, invisible */
.faded  { opacity: 0; }         /* space kept, still interactive */
deep:
The three are not interchangeable

  • display:none — the element is gone from the layout entirely. No box, no space, not focusable, ignored by screen readers. Toggling it reflows the page.
  • visibility:hidden — the element is invisible but still occupies its space (a hole in the layout). Not clickable, not read by AT.
  • opacity:0 — fully transparent but fully present: it takes space, is still clickable/focusable, and is announced by screen readers. Only opacity is animatable of the three.

Which to use

  • Remove from flow (menus, tabs): display:none.
  • Reserve space to avoid layout shift: visibility:hidden.
  • Fade in/out with a transition: animate opacity (add pointer-events:none so the invisible element isn't clickable).
.fade { opacity: 0; pointer-events: none; transition: opacity .2s; }
.fade.show { opacity: 1; pointer-events: auto; }

How to say it in an interview

“display:none removes the element and its space; visibility:hidden hides it but keeps the space; opacity:0 makes it transparent while still taking space and staying interactive — so I add pointer-events:none and use opacity when I want to animate a fade.”

question: What is float, and what problem does clearfix solve?
answer:
float pulls an element to the left/right and lets text/inline content wrap around it — its original purpose (e.g. an image inside a paragraph). Floated elements are taken out of normal flow, so a parent containing only floats collapses to zero height.

clearfix forces the parent to contain its floats. The modern one-liner is display: flow-root; the old trick is a clearing pseudo-element.
tip: For layout, use Flexbox/Grid — float is now mainly for wrapping text around an image.
code:
/* modern */ .parent { display: flow-root; }
/* legacy  */ .clearfix::after { content: ''; display: block; clear: both; }

question: What is the difference between overflow: visible, hidden, auto, scroll and clip?
answer:
• visible — default; content can overflow outside the element.
  • hidden — clips overflowing content with no scrollbars.
  • auto — shows scrollbars only when needed.
  • scroll — always shows scrollbars, even if content fits.
  • clip — clips content like hidden but disables scrolling entirely.
tip: Use overflow:auto for most scrollable containers instead of scroll.

question: What is the difference between width, min-width and max-width?
answer: width sets the preferred width. min-width prevents the element from becoming smaller than a given size. max-width prevents it from growing beyond a given size. Together they make layouts responsive while avoiding elements becoming too small or too large.
tip: max-width:100% is commonly used to keep images responsive.

question: Why does a Flexbox child sometimes overflow even though flex-shrink is enabled?
answer: Flex items have a default min-width:auto, which prevents them from shrinking smaller than their content. Long text or large elements can therefore overflow. Setting min-width:0 allows the item to shrink correctly.
tip: If text refuses to truncate inside a flex item, the first thing to try is min-width:0.

question: What is the difference between height and min-height?
answer: height fixes the element's height. If the content is larger, it may overflow. min-height sets the minimum height but allows the element to grow if more content is added. It is commonly used for responsive layouts and full-page sections.
tip: Prefer min-height for content containers because it adapts to varying content.

question: What does pointer-events: none do and when should you use it?
answer: pointer-events:none prevents an element from receiving mouse or touch events. Clicks pass through to the element underneath. It is useful for disabled overlays, decorative elements, loading screens, or invisible elements with opacity:0.
tip: When using opacity:0, also consider pointer-events:none to avoid invisible clickable elements.

question: When should you use transform: translate() instead of top/left positioning?
answer: transform: translate() moves an element visually without affecting document layout, making it ideal for animations and transitions. Changing top or left can trigger layout recalculations (reflow), making animations less efficient.
tip: For smooth animations, prefer transform because browsers can optimize it using the GPU.

question: What is the difference between CSS Transition and CSS Animation?
answer: Transition animates between two states when a property changes (for example on hover). Animation uses @keyframes and can run automatically, repeat infinitely, or contain multiple stages without user interaction.
tip: Use Transition for simple state changes and Animation for complex or repeating effects.

question: How do you display an ellipsis (...) when text overflows?
answer: Use the combination of white-space: nowrap, overflow: hidden, and text-overflow: ellipsis. All three properties are required for single-line text truncation.
tip: Many developers forget overflow:hidden, causing ellipsis not to appear.

question: How do you create a triangle using only CSS?
answer: A CSS triangle is created by giving an element zero width and height, then applying transparent borders on three sides and a colored border on the remaining side.
tip: This technique is commonly used for tooltips, dropdown arrows and speech bubbles.

question: What is the difference between display:none, visibility:hidden, opacity:0 and pointer-events:none?
answer:
• display:none removes the element from the layout.
  • visibility:hidden hides the element but still reserves its space.
  • opacity:0 makes the element invisible but it still occupies space and remains clickable.
  • pointer-events:none disables mouse/touch interaction while keeping the element visible (or invisible).
tip: An element with opacity:0 can still receive clicks unless pointer-events:none is also applied.
